1. # multi-model-skill
2. 
3. A Copilot CLI skill, with an optional repo-local extension, for cost-aware model routing.
4. 
5. ## What It Does
6. 
7. Routes Copilot CLI work to the lowest-cost viable model based on task type and complexity. The current extension also bumps tool-heavy implementation work from economy candidates to builder candidates.
8. 
9. - **Economy tier** → Quick Q&A, simple edits, lightweight exploration
10. - **Standard builder tier** → Normal implementation, multi-file work, tool-heavy implementation
11. - **Reasoning tier** → Complex planning, difficult debugging, explicit code review
12. 
13. ## Key Features
14. 
15. - ✅ **Optional automatic model switching** - The repo-local extension can switch models before each turn
16. - ✅ **Tool-aware implementation routing** - Tool keywords can move implementation work from economy to builder candidates
17. - ✅ **Cost-optimized** - Prefers cheaper models for lightweight work and escalates only when the classifier does
18. - ✅ **Explicit review only** - No forced post-implementation review; review mode only starts on explicit review phrases
19. - ✅ **Review diversity** - For explicit review, prefers a different model than the last implementation model when available
20. - ✅ **Graceful fallback** - If preferred model unavailable, tries next candidate in tier  
21. 
22. ## Installation
23. 
24. ### Option 1: Plugin Installation (Easiest)
25. 
26. Install the model-router as a Copilot CLI plugin:
27. 
28. ```bash
29. copilot plugin install kdaigle/multi-model-skill
30. ```
31. 
32. This installs the plugin for your current project. To list installed plugins:
33. 
34. ```bash
35. copilot /plugin list
36. ```
37. 
38. ### Option 2: Git Clone (Project-specific)
39. 
40. Install the skill for a single repository:
41. 
42. ```bash
43. # Clone the repository temporarily
44. git clone https://github.com/kdaigle/multi-model-skill.git /tmp/multi-model-skill
45. 
46. # Copy the skill to your project
47. mkdir -p .github/skills
48. cp -r /tmp/multi-model-skill/.github/skills/model-router .github/skills/
49. cp /tmp/multi-model-skill/.github/copilot-instructions.md .github/
50. 
51. # Optional: Copy the extension for automatic model switching
52. mkdir -p .github/extensions  
53. cp -r /tmp/multi-model-skill/.github/extensions/model-router .github/extensions/
54. 
55. # Clean up
56. rm -rf /tmp/multi-model-skill
57. ```
58. 
59. ### Option 3: Personal Installation
60. 
61. Install the skill globally for all your projects:
62. 
63. ```bash
64. # Clone the repository temporarily
65. git clone https://github.com/kdaigle/multi-model-skill.git /tmp/multi-model-skill
66. 
67. # Copy to your personal skills directory
68. mkdir -p ~/.copilot/skills
69. cp -r /tmp/multi-model-skill/.github/skills/model-router ~/.copilot/skills/
70. 
71. # Clean up
72. rm -rf /tmp/multi-model-skill
73. ```
74. 
75. ### After Installation
76. 
77. If Copilot CLI is already running, reload skills:
78. 
79. ```bash
80. /skills reload
81. ```
82. 
83. Verify installation:
84. 
85. ```bash
86. /skills list
87. /skills info model-router
88. ```
89. 
90. ### What Gets Installed
91. 
92. This repository contains:
93. - **Skill** (`.github/skills/model-router/`) — Routing guidance that Copilot can load when relevant, or when you invoke `/model-router`
94. - **Instructions** (`.github/copilot-instructions.md`) — Repo guidance for using the skill consistently
95. - **Optional extension** (`.github/extensions/model-router/`) — Repo-local hook that can switch models before each prompt
96. 
97. After copying the skill:
98. - Copilot can load it when the prompt matches the skill description
99. - You can invoke it explicitly with `/model-router`
100. - If you added the extension too, the extension can apply automatic model switching for this repository
101. 
102. ## How It Works
103. 
104. ### Skill (`SKILL.md`)
105. - Provides routing policy documentation and heuristics
106. - May be selected by Copilot when relevant to the prompt
107. - Can also be invoked explicitly with `/model-router`
108. 
109. ### Optional extension (`extension.mjs`)
110. - Runs as a subprocess communicating with Copilot CLI over JSON-RPC
111. - Runs the `onUserPromptSubmitted` hook before each message
112. - Classifies the prompt to determine task type and complexity
113. - Selects and switches models before sending to agent
114. - Tracks last implementation model to prefer different model for review
115. 
116. ### Routing Matrix (`references/routing-matrix.md`)
117. - Detailed task-to-model mapping
118. - Cost tier organization (Economy, Standard Builder, Reasoning)
119. - Tool-calling capability breakdown
120. - Diversity-of-thought rule for review
121. 
122. ## Tool-Aware Routing
123. 
124. The extension detects tool keywords and adds complexity for implementation prompts:
125. 
126. **Keywords detected:**
127. - Execution: `bash`, `curl`, `execute`, `run`, `script`, `command`, `shell`
128. - Integration: `api`, `http`, `endpoint`, `request`, `function`
129. - Orchestration: `tool`, `call`, `invoke`, `agent`
130. 
131. **Current routing behavior:**
132. - **Lightweight or non-implementation work** → usually stays in economy unless other complexity signals apply
133. - **Tool-heavy implementation** → moves to builder candidates
134. - **Planning, debugging, and explicit review** → may escalate to reasoning candidates based on the classifier
135. 
136. ## Model Coverage
137. 
138. ### Economy Tier
139. - `claude-haiku-4.5` (strong tool calling, best value)
140. - `gpt-4.1`
141. - `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5.4-mini`
142. 
143. ### Standard Builder Tier
144. - `claude-sonnet-4`, `claude-sonnet-4.5`
145. - `claude-sonnet-4.6` (builder-tier candidate with medium reasoning effort)
146. - `gpt-5.1`, `gpt-5.2`, `gpt-5.3-codex`, `gpt-5.1-codex`
147. 
148. ### Reasoning Tier
149. - `gpt-5`, `gpt-5.4`
150. - `claude-sonnet-4.6` (high-reasoning fallback for hard planning, debugging, or review)
151. - `claude-opus-4.5`, `claude-opus-4.6`, `claude-opus-4.6-1m`
152. - `gpt-5.1-codex-max`
153. 
154. ## Usage Examples
155. 
156. ### Simple Classification
157. ```
158. User: "What does this function do?"
159. → Economy tier (Haiku 4.5) — quick explanation
160. ```
161. 
162. ### Tool-Heavy Implementation
163. ```
164. User: "Write a bash script that fetches data from an API and processes it with curl"
165. → Routes to builder candidates because the implementation prompt is tool-heavy
166. ```
167. 
168. ### Explicit Code Review
169. ```
170. User: "Review my PR for bugs and security issues"
171. → Reasoning candidates first
172. → Uses different model than last implementation if available
173. ```
174. 
175. ## Evaluation
176. 
177. The `eval/` directory contains a local-first harness for comparing router-enabled runs against a fixed-model baseline on the same task. See [`eval/README.md`](eval/README.md) for local usage, result interpretation, and cost metrics. CI setup is documented in [`eval/docs/ci-setup.md`](eval/docs/ci-setup.md).
178. 
179. ## Files
180. 
181. ```
182. .github/
183. ├── skills/
184. │   └── model-router/
185. │       ├── SKILL.md                          # Skill definition & routing heuristics
186. │       └── references/
187. │           └── routing-matrix.md             # Detailed model tier & task mapping
188. ├── extensions/
189. │   └── model-router/
190. │       └── extension.mjs                     # Runtime extension for auto-switching
191. └── copilot-instructions.md                   # Instructions for skill activation
192. ```
193. 
194. ## How Routing Decisions Are Made
195. 
196. 1. **Classify the prompt**: Detect task type (implementation, planning, debugging, review, lightweight)
197. 2. **Score complexity**: Based on word count, keywords, and tool usage
198. 3. **Determine candidate tier**: Economy, builder, or reasoning based on task type + complexity
199. 4. **Select candidate model**: Choose from tier's model list in order of preference
200. 5. **Apply diversity rule**: For review, prefer different model than last implementation
201. 6. **Switch model**: Call `session.rpc.model.switchTo()` before the agent sees the prompt
202. 7. **Log decision**: Surface the choice via `session.log()` for transparency
203. 
204. ## Diversity-of-Thought Rule
205. 
206. When you explicitly ask for a code review:
207. - Prefers a different **model family** when possible (for example, Claude-based for build and GPT-based for review)
208. - Falls back to different concrete model in same family if needed
209. - Gracefully reuses current model if no alternative available
210. 
211. This aims to improve review quality by using different reasoning patterns when an alternative is available.
212. 
213. ## Complexity Scoring
214. 
215. Factors that increase complexity score:
216. - Prompt length (>40 words +1, >100 words +1)
217. - Keywords: multi-file, end-to-end, carefully, thorough, complex, large, entire codebase (+2)
218. - Task type: planning, debugging (+1)
219. - Tool keywords: bash, curl, api, execute (+1)
220. 
221. Higher scores escalate from economy → builder → reasoning tiers.
222. 
223. ## Explicit Review Only
224. 
225. **Important:** This router does NOT automatically review code after implementation.
226. 
227. Review mode triggers only on explicit phrases such as:
228. - "review", "code review", "review this", "review my code", "audit", "approval readiness", "approve this", "PR review"
229. 
230. Generic mentions of "bug" or "security" do not trigger review mode by themselves.
231. 
232. ## Extension Status
233. 
234. Check the extension status with the `/model_router_status` tool:
235. 
236. ```
237. /model_router_status
238. ```
239. 
240. Returns:
241. - Current model
242. - Last decision (task type, complexity, selected tier & model)
243. - Last implementation model (used for review diversity tracking)
244. - Last review model
245. 
246. ## Customization
247. 
248. To customize routing decisions, edit the skill or extension in the location where you placed it:
249. 
250. Then edit:
251. 
252. 1. **Task keywords**: Modify `PLAN_KEYWORDS`, `DEBUG_KEYWORDS`, `IMPLEMENT_KEYWORDS` in `extension.mjs`
253. 2. **Tool keywords**: Modify `TOOL_KEYWORDS` in `extension.mjs`
254. 3. **Model candidates**: Modify `MODEL_CANDIDATES` object in `extension.mjs`
255. 4. **Complexity scoring**: Adjust `getComplexity()` function in `extension.mjs`
256. 5. **Routing policy**: Update `references/routing-matrix.md` with new task-to-model mappings
257. 
258. After changing the skill during a live CLI session, reload skills:
259. ```bash
260. /skills reload
261. ```
262. 
263. If you changed the extension, restart the relevant Copilot CLI session so the repo-local extension is reloaded.
264. 
265. ## Technical Details
266. 
267. - **Extension framework**: GitHub Copilot SDK for Node.js
268. - **Communication**: JSON-RPC over stdio (no console.log; use `session.log()`)
269. - **Hook**: `onUserPromptSubmitted` fires before each user message
270. - **Model switching**: `session.rpc.model.switchTo({ modelId, reasoningEffort })`
271. - **Reasoning effort**: The extension requests `low`, `medium`, or `high` where supported
272. 
273. ## License
274. 
275. MIT
276. 
277. ## Contributing
278. 
279. Improvements welcome! Consider:
280. - Adding more task type detection
281. - Tuning complexity scoring
282. - Expanding tool keyword detection
283. - Testing against real-world prompts
284. 
285. ---
286. 
287. **Repository:** https://github.com/kdaigle/multi-model-skill
288.

 - "review", "code review", "review this", "review my code", "audit", "approval readiness", "approve this", "PR review"
 
 Generic mentions of "bug" or "security" do not trigger review mode by themselves.
 
 ## Extension Status
 
 Check the extension status with the `/model_router_status` tool:
 
 ```
 /model_router_status
 ```
 
 Returns:
 - Current model
 - Last decision (task type, complexity, selected tier & model)
 - Last implementation model (used for review diversity tracking)
 - Last review model
 
 ## Customization
 
 To customize routing decisions, edit the skill or extension in the location where you placed it:
 
 Then edit:
 
 1. **Task keywords**: Modify `PLAN_KEYWORDS`, `DEBUG_KEYWORDS`, `IMPLEMENT_KEYWORDS` in `extension.mjs`
 2. **Tool keywords**: Modify `TOOL_KEYWORDS` in `extension.mjs`
 3. **Model candidates**: Modify `MODEL_CANDIDATES` object in `extension.mjs`
 4. **Complexity scoring**: Adjust `getComplexity()` function in `extension.mjs`
 5. **Routing policy**: Update `references/routing-matrix.md` with new task-to-model mappings
 
 After changing the skill during a live CLI session, reload skills:
 ```bash
 /skills reload
 ```
 
 If you changed the extension, restart the relevant Copilot CLI session so the repo-local extension is reloaded.
 
 ## Technical Details
 
 - **Extension framework**: GitHub Copilot SDK for Node.js
 - **Communication**: JSON-RPC over stdio (no console.log; use `session.log()`)
 - **Hook**: `onUserPromptSubmitted` fires before each user message
 - **Model switching**: `session.rpc.model.switchTo({ modelId, reasoningEffort })`
 - **Reasoning effort**: The extension requests `low`, `medium`, or `high` where supported

 ## Contributing
 
 Improvements welcome! Consider:
 - Adding more task type detection
 - Tuning complexity scoring
 - Expanding tool keyword detection
 - Testing against real-world prompts

- Removed `"orchestrate"` and `"orchestration"` from the orchestration keyword list — they were quietly dropped from the runtime (`ORCHESTRATION_KEYWORDS`) because bare mentions in documentation context caused false positives
- Expanded the tool-keyword paragraph to list the full `TOOL_KEYWORDS` array (`call`, `invoke`, `script`, `command`, `shell`, `http`, `request`, `endpoint` were previously omitted)

**`README.md`**
- Replaced the misleading three-group layout (Execution / Integration / **Orchestration**) in the Tool-Aware Routing section with two clearly-labeled lists:
  - **Tool-implementation keywords** — single words that push implementation work to the builder tier
  - **Orchestration keywords** — multi-word phrases that escalate to the reasoning tier
  
  The old grouping labelled `tool`, `call`, `invoke`, `agent` as "Orchestration" keywords, incorrectly implying they trigger reasoning-tier escalation; they are actually `TOOL_KEYWORDS` that only bump builder-tier selection. Added an explicit bullet for orchestration-prompt escalation to reasoning so the distinction is clear.
